title: Sci-Fi Writer Arthur C. Clarke Predicts the Future in 1964: Artificial Intelligence, Instantaneous Global Communication, Remote Work, Singularity & More
description:
in Science, Technology |   	April 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Are you feel┬Łing con┬Łfi┬Łdent about the future? No? We under┬Łstand. Would you
body:
in Science, Technology |   	April 13th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Are you feel┬Łing con┬Łfi┬Łdent about the future? No? We under┬Łstand. Would you like to know what it was like to feel a deep cer┬Łtain┬Łty that the decades to come were going to be filled with won┬Łder and the fan┬Łtas┬Łtic? Well then, gaze upon this clip from the BBC Archive YouTube chan┬Łnel of sci-fi author Arthur C. Clarke pre┬Łdict┬Łing the future in 1964.
Although we best know him for writ┬Łing 2001: A Space Odyssey, the 1964 tele┬Łvi┬Łsion-view┬Łing pub┬Łlic would have known him for his futur┬Łism and his tal┬Łent for calm┬Łly explain┬Łing all the great things to come. In the late 1940s, he had already pre┬Łdict┬Łed telecom┬Łmu┬Łni┬Łca┬Łtion satel┬Łlites. In 1962 he pub┬Łlished his col┬Łlect┬Łed essays, Pro┬Łfiles of the Future, which con┬Łtains many of the ideas in this clip.
Here he cor┬Łrect┬Łly pre┬Łdicts the ease with which we can be con┬Łtact┬Łed wher┬Łev┬Łer in the world we choose to, where we can con┬Łtact our friends ŌĆ£any┬Łwhere on earth even if we donŌĆÖt know their loca┬Łtion.ŌĆØ What Clarke doesnŌĆÖt pre┬Łdict here is how ŌĆ£loca┬ŁtionŌĆØ isnŌĆÖt a thing when weŌĆÖre on the inter┬Łnet. He imag┬Łines peo┬Łple work┬Łing just as well from Tahi┬Łti or Bali as they do from Lon┬Łdon. Clarke sees this advance┬Łment as the down┬Łfall of the mod┬Łern city, as we do not need to com┬Łmute into the city to work. Now, as so many of us are doing our jobs from home post-COVID, weŌĆÖve also dis┬Łcov┬Łered the dystopia in that fan┬Łta┬Łsy. (It cer┬Łtain┬Łly has┬ŁnŌĆÖt dropped the cost of rent.)
Next, he pre┬Łdicts advances in biotech┬Łnol┬Ło┬Łgy that would allow us to, say, train mon┬Łkeys to work as ser┬Łvants and work┬Łers. (Until, he jokes, they form a union and ŌĆ£weŌĆÖd be back right where we start┬Łed.) Per┬Łhaps, he says, humans have stopped evolvingŌĆöwhat comes next is arti┬Łfi┬Łcial intel┬Łli┬Łgence (although that phrase had yet to be used) and machine evo┬Łlu┬Łtion, where weŌĆÖd be hon┬Łored to be the ŌĆ£step┬Łping stoneŌĆØ towards that des┬Łtiny. Make of that what you will. I know you might think it would be cool to have a mon┬Łkey but┬Łler, but cŌĆÖmon, think of the ethics, not to men┬Łtion the cost of bananas.
Point┬Łing out where Clarke gets it wrong is too easyŌĆönobody gets it right all of the time. How┬Łev┬Łer, it is fas┬Łci┬Łnat┬Łing that some things that have nev┬Łer come to passŌĆöbeing able to learn a lan┬Łguage overnight, or eras┬Łing your memoriesŌĆöhave man┬Łaged to resur┬Łface over the years as sci┬Łence fic┬Łtion films, like Eter┬Łnal Sun┬Łshine of the Spot┬Łless Mind. His ideas of cryo┬Łgenic sus┬Łpen┬Łsion are sta┬Łples of numer┬Łous hard sci-fi films.
And we are still wait┬Łing for the ŌĆ£Repli┬Łca┬ŁtorŌĆØ machine, which would make exact dupli┬Łcates of objects (and by so doing cause a col┬Łlapse into ŌĆ£glut┬Łto┬Łnous bar┬ŁbarismŌĆØ because weŌĆÖd want unlim┬Łit┬Łed amounts of every┬Łthing.) Some com┬Łmenters call this a pre┬Łcur┬Łsor to 3ŌĆæD print┬Łing. IŌĆÖd say oth┬Łer┬Łwise, but some┬Łthing very close to it might be around the cor┬Łner. Who knows? Clarke him┬Łself agrees about all this conjectureŌĆöitŌĆÖs doomed to fail.
ŌĆ£That is why the future is so end┬Łless┬Łly fas┬Łci┬Łnat┬Łing. Try as we can, weŌĆÖll nev┬Łer out┬Łguess it.ŌĆØ
Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2022.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
How French Artists in 1899 Envi┬Łsioned What Life Would Look Like in the Year 2000┬Ā
Isaac Asi┬Łmov Pre┬Łdicts the Future on┬ĀThe David Let┬Łter┬Łman Show┬Ā(1980)
In 1926, Niko┬Łla Tes┬Łla Pre┬Łdicts the World of 2026
In 1922, a Nov┬Łel┬Łist Pre┬Łdicts What the World Will Look Like in 2022: Wire┬Łless Tele┬Łphones, 8ŌĆæHour Flights to Europe & More
In 1894, A French Writer Pre┬Łdict┬Łed the End of Books & the Rise of Portable Audio┬Łbooks and Pod┬Łcasts
Ted Mills is a free┬Łlance writer on the arts.

title: Isaac Asimov Reviews George OrwellŌĆÖs Nineteen Eighty-Four and Calls It ŌĆ£Not Science Fiction, But a Distorted Nostalgia for a Past that Never WasŌĆØ
description: Here in the twen┬Łty-twen┬Łties,┬Āa young read┬Łer first hear┬Łing of George OrwellŌĆÖs Nine┬Łteen Eighty-Four would hard┬Łly imag┬Łine it to be a work of
body:
Here in the twen┬Łty-twen┬Łties,┬Āa young read┬Łer first hear┬Łing of George OrwellŌĆÖs Nine┬Łteen Eighty-Four would hard┬Łly imag┬Łine it to be a work of sci┬Łence fic┬Łtion. That would┬ŁnŌĆÖt have been the case in 1949, when the nov┬Łel was first pub┬Łlished, and when the epony┬Łmous year would have sound┬Łed like the dis┬Łtant future. Even as the actu┬Łal nine┬Łteen-eight┬Łies came around, it still evoked visions of a tech┬Łno-total┬Łi┬Łtar┬Łi┬Łan dystopia ahead. ŌĆ£So thor┬Łough┬Łly has 1984-opho┬Łbia pen┬Łe┬Łtrat┬Łed the con┬Łscious┬Łness of many who have not read the book and have no notion of what it con┬Łtains, that one won┬Łders what will hap┬Łpen to us after 31 Decem┬Łber 1984,ŌĆØ wrote Isaac Asi┬Łmov in 1980. ŌĆ£When New YearŌĆÖs Day of 1985 arrives and the Unit┬Łed States is still in exis┬Łtence and fac┬Łing very much the prob┬Łlems it faces today, how will we express our fears of what┬Łev┬Łer aspect of life fills us with appre┬Łhen┬Łsion?ŌĆØ
The occa┬Łsion was one of a series of syn┬Łdi┬Łcat┬Łed news┬Łpa┬Łper columns that Asi┬Łmov seems to have pub┬Łlished each new year. At the dawn of Nine┬Łteen Eighty-FourŌĆÖs decade, the syn┬Łdi┬Łcate asked him to revis┬Łit OrwellŌĆÖs nov┬Łel, which had already been a com┬Łmon cul┬Łtur┬Łal ref┬Łer┬Łence for decades. As a work of sci┬Łence fic┬Łtion (the genre for which his own name had prac┬Łti┬Łcal┬Łly come to stand), he finds it lack┬Łing, to say the least. ŌĆ£The Lon┬Łdon in which the sto┬Łry is placed is not so much moved thir┬Łty-five years for┬Łward in time, from 1949 to 1984, as it is moved a thou┬Łsand miles east in space to Moscow,ŌĆØ he writes. Far from attempt┬Łing to imag┬Łine the future, in Asi┬ŁmovŌĆÖs view, Orwell sim┬Łply con┬Łvert┬Łed the Eng┬Łland he knew into a drea┬Łry Stal┬Łin┬Łist-type state. Apart from cer┬Łtain implau┬Łsi┬Łble sur┬Łveil┬Łlance sys┬Łtems, the set┬Łting is ŌĆ£incred┬Łi┬Łbly old-fash┬Łioned when com┬Łpared with the real world of the 1980s.ŌĆØ
Orwell does┬ŁnŌĆÖt even both┬Łer to imag┬Łine any new vices: ŌĆ£His char┬Łac┬Łters are all gin hounds and tobac┬Łco addicts,ŌĆØ Asi┬Łmov writes, ŌĆ£and part of the hor┬Łror of his pic┬Łture of 1984 is his elo┬Łquent descrip┬Łtion of the low qual┬Łi┬Łty of the gin and tobac┬Łco.ŌĆØ That telling detail hints at one of OrwellŌĆÖs major sources of inspi┬Łra┬Łtion: the British Min┬Łistry of Infor┬Łma┬Łtion, his wifeŌĆÖs employ┬Łer dur┬Łing World War II, and the source of the mate┬Łr┬Łi┬Łal he broad┬Łcast to India while work┬Łing at the BBC around the same time.┬Ā The Min┬ŁistryŌĆÖs can┬Łteen, accord┬Łing to his let┬Łters, was not of the high┬Łest stan┬Łdard. WhatŌĆÖs more, the 850-word ŌĆ£Basic Eng┬ŁlishŌĆØ that it insist┬Łed on using in its broad┬Łcasts bears more than a pass┬Łing resem┬Łblance to Nine┬Łteen Eight-FourŌĆÖs Newspeak, the pared-down lan┬Łguage devel┬Łoped and man┬Łdat┬Łed by the gov┬Łern┬Łment in order to lim┬Łit its cit┬Łi┬ŁzensŌĆÖ range of thought.
Asi┬Łmov does┬ŁnŌĆÖt buy that either. ŌĆ£There is no sign that such com┬Łpres┬Łsions of the lan┬Łguage have ever weak┬Łened it as a mode of expres┬Łsion,ŌĆØ he writes. ŌĆ£As a mat┬Łter of fact, polit┬Łi┬Łcal obfus┬Łca┬Łtion has tend┬Łed to use many words rather than few, long words rather than short, to extend rather than to reduce.ŌĆØ (This, of course, was some┬Łthing Orwell knew.) What┬Łev┬Łer Nine┬Łteen Eighty-FourŌĆÖs short┬Łcom┬Łings as prophe┬Łcy, sci-fi, or indeed lit┬Łer┬Ła┬Łture, Asi┬Łmov does cred┬Łit Orwell with a cer┬Łtain geopo┬Łlit┬Łi┬Łcal savvy. Its world-rul┬Łing trio of Ocea┬Łnia, Eura┬Łsia, and Eas┬Łt┬Ła┬Łsia ŌĆ£fits in, very rough┬Łly, with the three actu┬Łal super┬Łpow┬Łers of the 1980s: the Unit┬Łed States, the Sovi┬Łet Union, and Chi┬Łna.ŌĆØ Orwell knew, as many did┬ŁnŌĆÖt, that the lat┬Łter two would not join forces, per┬Łhaps thanks to his own frus┬Łtrat┬Łing expe┬Łri┬Łence fight┬Łing for fac┬Łtion┬Łal┬Łism-prone left caus┬Łes. But not even as future-ori┬Łent┬Łed a mind as Asi┬ŁmovŌĆÖs would have guessed that, just a few years lat┬Łer, the USSR would be out of the game ŌĆö and a few decades lat┬Łer, the word Orwellian┬Āwould be applied most often to Chi┬Łna.
Read Asi┬ŁmovŌĆÖs take on 1984 here.
If you would like to sup┬Łport the mis┬Łsion of Open Cul┬Łture, con┬Łsid┬Łer mak┬Łing┬Āa dona┬Łtion to our site. ItŌĆÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬Łtri┬Łbu┬Łtions will help us con┬Łtin┬Łue pro┬Łvid┬Łing the best free cul┬Łtur┬Łal and edu┬Łca┬Łtion┬Łal mate┬Łri┬Łals to learn┬Łers every┬Łwhere. You can con┬Łtribute through Pay┬ŁPal, Patre┬Łon, and Ven┬Łmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
An Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Intro┬Łduc┬Łtion to George Orwell
An Intro┬Łduc┬Łtion to George OrwellŌĆÖs 1984 and How Pow┬Łer Man┬Łu┬Łfac┬Łtures Truth
George Orwell Explains in a Reveal┬Łing 1944 Let┬Łter Why HeŌĆÖd Write 1984
George OrwellŌĆÖs Har┬Łrow┬Łing Race to Fin┬Łish 1984 Before His Death
Isaac Asi┬Łmov Pre┬Łdicts in 1964 What the World Will Look Like in 2014
Rid┬Łley Scott on the Mak┬Łing of AppleŌĆÖs Icon┬Łic ŌĆ£1984ŌĆØ Com┬Łmer┬Łcial, Aired on Super Bowl Sun┬Łday in 1984
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Ai Weiwei and the Art of Keeping Your Mouth Shut
description:
Book Review
                ŌĆ£On CensorshipŌĆØ offers timely reflections from the dissident artist, whose entire life and career ha
body:
Book Review
                ŌĆ£On CensorshipŌĆØ offers timely reflections from the dissident artist, whose entire life and career have been marked by state persecution.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Cover of On Censorship by Ai Weiwei (Thames & Hudson, 2026)ItŌĆÖs hard to maintain dignity and self-respect when your mouth is gagged. You keep your head low, lips tight, and social media feed squeaky clean. You distrust friends and colleagues you used to run your mouth to. You feel temporary relief when itŌĆÖs not you whoŌĆÖs snatched from bed in the middle of the night and sent to a remote detention camp. But you know youŌĆÖre living on borrowed time: How long can you silence the very thing that makes you human?┬ĀCensorship, writes dissident Chinese artist Ai Weiwei in a small but mighty new book, ŌĆ£strips innocence from the young and kindness from the elderly. It discourages people from valuing justice, fostering selfishness instead.ŌĆØ┬Ā┬ĀOn Censorship (2026) packs in ever-timely reflections from an artist whose entire life and career have been marked by state persecution. As a child, he saw his father, poet and activist Ai Qing, clean communal toilets in a labor camp. As an adult, his exhibitions were axed, his reputation was tarnished, and his studio was raided and surveilled. And when that wasnŌĆÖt enough to silence him, the ruling Communist Party detained him for 81 days without charge in 2011. He was later condemned to a life of exile. More recently, authorities even made sure the Chinese AI chatbot DeepSeek would forget him.┬Ā┬ĀIn the early days of IsraelŌĆÖs genocide in Gaza, Ai learned the hard way that sheltering in the West had its limitations, too. In November 2023, Lisson Gallery canceled his show at its London location after he posted his thoughts about the conflict on social media. For a moment, one of the worldŌĆÖs biggest artists became as powerless as the university students being penalized for protesting for Palestinian rights.ŌĆ£In the western world, censorship alone cannot achieve its goals; it relies heavily on self-censorship,ŌĆØ he explains, reminding people in self-styled democracies that theyŌĆÖre not safe, either.┬ĀSelf-censorship, this silent killer, is perhaps the most soul-eroding part of life under an authoritarian regime. In a distorted, upside-down world of fear and inhibition, war is peace, submission is freedom, and ignorance is strength.┬ĀWe end up with a society that is ŌĆ£numb, devoid of emotional depth, perception and creativity,ŌĆØ and that is ŌĆ£marked by self-mockery and self-consolation,ŌĆØ warns Ai.┬ĀWhat else could we call the recent tragic example of filmmaker Wim Wenders, who twisted himself into pretzels while trying to answer a question about the Berlin International Film Festival's silence on Gaza? You could see the shock and misery, almost grief, on his face when he realized that the words ŌĆ£we have to stay out of politicsŌĆØ had just left his mouth.┬ĀTo be fair to Wenders, how many of us have pursed our lips at the bitter aftertaste of biting our tongues and swallowing our words over the past two and a half years? How many of us have played dumb to keep out of trouble?┬Ā┬Ā┬ĀThe most useful part of On Censorship is a reminder that the power to reclaim freedom of speech is in our hands. Censorship and surveillance only work if we participate in them. The moment we stop fearing it, the system will collapse all at once.On Censorship (2026) by Ai Weiwei is published by Thames & Hudson and is available online and through independent booksellers.

title: Jeremy Frey: The Generational Impact of a New Artistic Path
description:
Member Event
                Join us on April 29 for a conversation with artist and recent MacArthur Fellowship winner Jeremy Fr
body:
Member Event
                Join us on April 29 for a conversation with artist and recent MacArthur Fellowship winner Jeremy Frey and Hyperallergic Editor-at-Large Hrag Vartanian.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Jeremy Frey (Photo John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation CC BY-NC 4.0)
    
        
            Jeremy Frey is one of the most celebrated Indigenous weavers in the country. Hailing from Maine, he learned traditional Wabanaki weaving techniques from his mother and through apprenticeships with the Maine Indian Basketmakers Alliance. His art balances tradition with innovation, while imbuing each object with new ideas and creating a market for objects that havenŌĆÖt traditionally been part of the contemporary art gallery ecosystem. Through experimentation, he has forged a singular aesthetic that embraces the fusion of craft, design, and contemporary art, while being influenced by forms of every kind and developing his own techniques that push the artform to new levels.Frey was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship in 2025, and he will join Hyperallergic co-founder and editor-at-large Hrag Vartanian to talk about how he shepherds each artwork that begins with harvesting his own materials and ending with works that have challenged traditional categorization and been exhibited in the world's most renowned art museums.This event is free for Hyperallergic paid members.

                        The Generational Impact of Jeremy FreyŌĆÖs New Artistic PathWednesday April 29, 3ŌĆō4pm (ET), Online
                        
                            Join now to register
                        
                    
Jeremy Frey┬Ā(b. 1978, Passamaquoddy Indian Township Reservation, Maine) is one of the foremost Passamaquoddy craftspeople of his generation. A descendant of a long line of Indigenous weavers,┬ĀFrey┬Ālearned traditional Wabanaki methods from his mother and by apprenticing at the Maine Indian Basketmakers Alliance. Woven from natural materials that the artist himself forages, such as sweetgrass and wood from black ash trees,┬ĀFreyŌĆÖs vessels are characterized by subtle forms, delicately layered colors, and elaborate weaves. Building on and experimenting with the material histories of Wabanaki basketry, his work is also in dialogue with contemporary sculptureŌĆÖs emphasis on materiality, form, and variation within repetition. To create his basket relief prints,┬ĀFrey┬Āhas developed a novel form of flat weaving that can be run repeatedly through a printing press, preserving and sharing his techniques without impacting the stock of his rare materials.┬ĀFrey┬Ālives in Maine.Hrag Vartanian is editor-at-large, founding editor, and co-founder of Hyperallergic.Previously on HyperallergicThe Mesmerizing Wonder of Wabanaki WeavingJeremy FreyŌĆÖs first museum show presents the artistŌĆÖs virtuosic craft as must-see contemporary work.HyperallergicJulie Schneider

                        Jeremy Frey

title: What Is a "Post-Duchamp" Art World?
description:
Daily Newsletter
                Art fair dispatches from Chicago to S├Żo Paulo, Thierry de Duve on Duchamp at MoMA, and Ela Troy
body:
Daily Newsletter
                Art fair dispatches from Chicago to S├Żo Paulo, Thierry de Duve on Duchamp at MoMA, and Ela Troyano remembers Agosto Machado.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Like a hall of mirrors or a Borgesian labyrinth, Duchamp's oeuvre asks us to look at the past reflecting endlessly into the future. In an interview today, scholar Thierry de Duve praises a kind of mise en abyme in MoMA's new Duchamp show, which features not one but seven ŌĆ£bo├«tes-en-valiseŌĆØ ŌĆö the artist's clever miniature exhibitions-in-a-box, created decades before his first museum retrospective. This is "Duchamp's typical genius," de Duve tells Associate Editor Lisa Yin Zhang. ŌĆ£I think he anticipated the logic of the museum.ŌĆØ Read their conversation, in which de Duve muses on what it means to live in a ŌĆ£post-DuchampŌĆØ world.Also today, our writers bring you art-fair dispatches from Chicago to S├Żo Paulo and back to New York, where prints abound. And if you're around this Wednesday, April 15, join MacArthur "Genius" grant winner Tonika Lewis Johnson and me for a virtual talk on her social practice art and unlearning the logic of segregation.ŌĆöValentina Di Liscia, senior editor
From or by Marcel Duchamp or Rrose S├®lavy, ŌĆ£Box in a ValiseŌĆØ (1935ŌĆō1941) (photo Lisa Yin Zhang/Hyperallergic)Marcel Duchamp Was the Messenger of HistoryŌĆ£The art world changed,ŌĆØ scholar Thierry de Duve told us on the occasion of MoMAŌĆÖs new show. ŌĆ£DuchampŌĆÖs ŌĆśFountainŌĆÖ is the message that brings us the news.ŌĆØ | Lisa Yin Zhang
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            In Kyoung Chun: Make RoomTransparent houses, suspended structures, and intimate paintings serve as metaphors for belonging in this exhibition at the Halsey Institute of Contemporary Art at the College of Charleston.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            Art Fairs Regina Jose╠ü Galindo, ŌĆ£Primavera Democra╠üticaŌĆØ (ŌĆ£Democratic SpringŌĆØ) (2025), on view at the 22nd edition of SP-Arte (photo Ela Bittencourt/Hyperallergic)The Paradoxical Delights of South AmericaŌĆÖs Biggest Art FairThe 22nd edition of SP-Arte in S├Żo Paulo stands at a global nexus, yet feels decidedly regional. | Ela BittencourtWho Do ChicagoŌĆÖs Art Fairs Serve?Expo Chicago and its orbit of shows reveal both the joys and pain points of the cityŌĆÖs current creative environment. | Natalie JenkinsProcess Is the Point at IFPDA Print FairŌĆ£Print is a more democratic medium,ŌĆØ said Temma Nanas of Leslie Sacks Gallery, one of around 80 global galleries returning to the Park Avenue Armory for the annual fair. | Aaron ShortThe Brooklyn Fine Art Print Fair Has Taken Off Its Training WheelsShaking off any initial caution from last yearŌĆÖs beta test, it has charged forward and made itself a space to showcase the radical history and present of printmaking. | Rhea Nayyar
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Summer Marathons in Painting and Drawing at the New York Studio SchoolJoin an immersive, two-week session and transform your studio practice. Online or in-person.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            Queer IconsAgosto Machado in a 2003 glamour shot for his annual holiday card (┬® Uzi Parnes)Remembering Agosto Machado, Keeper of Queer HistoriesThe late performer and archivist spent decades as the quiet holder of our secrets, always behind the scenes, always a connector. | Ela TroyanoA View From the EaselStudio of Katya Granova, Leipzig, GermanyThis week, artist Billy Biondi blends painting and dancing, and Katya Granova works inside a former yarn factory.Want to take part? Check out our┬Āsubmission guidelines┬Āand share a bit about your studio with us through┬Āthis form! All mediums and workspaces are welcome, including your home studio.Michael Szpakowski on John Yau's ŌĆ£Jasper Johns Keeps LookingŌĆØ:Tremendous piece, packed with ideas & insight. (and *what* a stunning artist Johns is!)

                        Commenting privileges are reserved for paid members. Join us today!
                    
From the ArchiveMarcel Duchamp, ŌĆ£Dada 1916ŌĆō1923 / Sidney JanisŌĆØ (1953), detail (courtesy Swann Auction Galleries)The Dada Catalogue Marcel Duchamp Designed to Be Thrown AwayFor a 1953 Dada exhibition, Marcel Duchamp designed a one-page catalogue meant to be crumpled up and tossed in the trash. | Allison Meier

title: SothebyŌĆÖs Tries Again to Sell $40 M. Picasso Painting That DidnŌĆÖt Make It to Auction in 2008
description: A $40 million Pablo Picasso painting from the artistŌĆÖs Cubist period will hit the block at SothebyŌĆÖs this May during the marquee New York auctions.
body:
A $40 million Pablo Picasso painting from the artistŌĆÖs Cubist period will hit the block at SothebyŌĆÖs this May during the marquee New York auctions.

	Part of a cache of works from the collection of late Surrealist artist Enrico Donati and his wife Adele, who died last year, Arlequin (Buste), from 1909, is likely to be one of the most expensive works by Picasso sold during the marquee sales this season. It features a harlequin that appears to emerge from a cascade of three-dimensional geometric forms.

	
		Related Articles
	


	The auction house said on Monday that the work was valued within ŌĆ£the region of $40 million,ŌĆØ suggesting that it has increased dramatically in monetary worth since Enrico bought it for around $12,000 in the 1940s. He purchased it through Daniel-Henry Kahnweiler, one of PicassoŌĆÖs most famous dealers, having become enamored of the piece upon seeing it at the Mus├®e National dŌĆÖArt Moderne in Paris.
	

	ItŌĆÖs the second high-profile attempt by SothebyŌĆÖs to sell Arlequin, which made its way to market at the auction house in 2008, not long after Enrico died. Then valued at more than $30 million, the painting was unexpectedly withdrawn just a week before a November Impressionist and modern art auction by SothebyŌĆÖs, which stated only that the work would not make it to the block for ŌĆ£private reasons.ŌĆØ 

	At the time, according to the New York Times, SothebyŌĆÖs was selling the work without a guarantee, an agreement that entitles the seller to a sum whether or not the work finds a buyer. ChristieŌĆÖs, the houseŌĆÖs main rival, reportedly offered the Donati estate a guarantee, plus ŌĆ£other types of financing.ŌĆØ But the work headed to SothebyŌĆÖs, anyway, with the stipulation that its consignor could remove it from auction.

	This time, the painting has a guarantee and an irrevocable bid, a SothebyŌĆÖs spokesperson told ARTnews. The painting heads to sale in an auction of modern art on May 19.

	Thirteen other works from the Donati collection are also headed to auction at SothebyŌĆÖs, including two more paintings that will appear in the May 19 sale. The first, a 1925 Wassily Kandinsky abstraction called Rote Tiefe (Red Depth), has a $12 millionŌĆō$18 million estimate. The second, a 1939 Yves Tanguy work titled Aux Aguets le jour, has a $800,000ŌĆō$1.2 million estimate.
	

	Meanwhile, an untitled Alexander Calder stabile with a high estimate of $1 million is coming to a May 20 day sale of modern art, and two masksŌĆöone by a YupŌĆÖik or Inupiaq artist, the other by a Bete-Guro artist, both from the 19th centuryŌĆöwill be auctioned in a June 18 sale for art from Africa, Oceania, and the Americas.

title: New YorkŌĆÖs Newest Triennial Lines Up 39 Artists for Star-Studded First Edition Along the Erie Canal
description: The Medina Triennial, a new recurring art exhibition set to launch in the titular Western New York village this summer, has revealed the artist list
body:
The Medina Triennial, a new recurring art exhibition set to launch in the titular Western New York village this summer, has revealed the artist list for its inaugural edition, due to open on June 6 in close proximity to the Erie Canal. The 39 participants include a range of artists well-known on the biennial circuit. 

	Lina Lapelyt─Ś, one of the artistŌĆÖs behind the Golden LionŌĆōwinning Lithuanian Pavilion at the 2019 Venice Biennale, is taking part, staging Faithfully Recording, through which local singers and construction workers will build a sculpture together. 
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	Taysir Batniji, a Palestinian photographer, will count the Medina Triennial as his third biennial this year, after appearances in ones held in Saudi Arabia and Sydney, and Tania Candiani, a Mexican artist, will stage a new commission called Two Waters, one of many works here dealing with bodies of water.

	Artist with local ties are also taking part. Asad Raza, a Berlin-based artist born in nearby Buffalo, will stage a new work in which he will redirect water from the Erie Canal to one of the showŌĆÖs venues, and Jay Carrier (Onondaga/Tuscarora Nations, Wolf Clan), a Niagara FallsŌĆōbased painter who died in 2025, will also appear here.

	Kari Conte and Karin Laansoo are serving as co-artistic director of the Medina Triennial, which this year takes the theme ŌĆ£All That Sustains Us.ŌĆØ They attributed its focus on ecology and sustainability to Mierle Laderman Ukeles, one of the exhibitionŌĆÖs participants.

	ŌĆ£What excites us most about this inaugural edition is that more than a year of conversations with artists and Medina residents will finally take public form. The exhibition has grown out of those exchanges and out of the village itself,ŌĆØ Conte and Laansoo said in an email to ARTnews.

	Moreover, they said, ŌĆ£We hope visitors will feel what the artists have felt: that Medina is not simply the setting for the Triennial, but its central animating force.ŌĆØ

	The full artist list follows below.

	Ash Arder (she/they) b. 1988, Flint, MI; lives in Detroit, MI

	Selva Aparicio (she/her) b. 1987, Barcelona, Spain; lives in Alfred, NY, and Chicago, IL

	James Beckett (he/him) b. 1977, Harare, Zimbabwe; lives in New York, NY and Amsterdam, Netherlands
	

	Taysir Batniji (he/him) b. 1966, Gaza, Palestine; lives in Paris, France

	Alice Bucknell (they/them) b. 1993, London, UK; lives in Los Angeles, CA

	Tania Candiani (she/her) b.1974, Mexico City, Mexico; lives in Mexico City, Mexico

	Jay Carrier (he/him) Onondaga/Tuscarora Nations, Wolf Clan; b. 1963, Six Nations reservation in Ontario, Canada; d. 2025, Niagara Falls, NY

	FIBRA est. 2019, Lima, Peru; Lucia Monge, b. 1983; Gianine Tabja, b.1983; Gabriela Flores del Pozo, b. 1979

	Harun Farocki (he/him) b. 1944, Nov├Į Ji─Ź├Łn, Czechoslovakia (present-day Czechia); d. 2014, Berlin, Germany

	Futurefarmers est. 1994, San Francisco, CA; including Amy Franceschini, b. 1970, Patterson, CA; Michael Swaine, b. 1971, Buffalo, NY; and FS B├Āss├»b├®t, b. 1997, Elmina, Ghana

	Jeneen Frei Njootli (they/them) b. 1988, Vuntut Gwitchin First Nation, Whitehorse, Yukon, Canada; lives in Old Crow, Yukon, Canada

	Greg Halpern (he/him) b. 1977, Buffalo, NY; lives in Rochester, NY

	Carole Harris (she/her) b. 1943, Detroit, MI; lives in Detroit, MI

	Scott Hocking (he/him) b. 1975, Detroit, MI; lives in Detroit, MI

	G├Čzde ─░lkin (they/them) b. 1981, K├╝tahya, T├╝rkiye; lives in ─░stanbul, T├╝rkiye
	

	AKI INOMATA (she/her) b. 1983, Tokyo, Japan; lives in Tokyo, Japan

	Richard Ibghy (he/him) & Marilou Lemmens (she/her) b. 1964, Montreal, Canada; lives in Durham-Sud, Canada b. 1976, Ascot Corner, Canada; lives in Durham-Sud, Canada

	K├żrt Ojavee (she/her) b. 1982, Rakvere, Estonia; lives in Tallinn, Estonia

	Anne Duk Hee Jordan (they/them) b.1978, South Korea; lives in Berlin, Germany

	Evelyne Leblanc-Roberge (she/they) b. 1981, Maria, Quebec, Canada; lives in Rochester, NY

	Lina Lapelyt─Ś (she/her) b. 1984, Kaunas, Lithuania; lives in London, UK, and Vilnius, Lithuania

	Matthew L├│pez-Jensen (he/him) b. 1980, CT; lives in Bronx, NY

	Cathy Lu (she/her) b. 1984, Miami, FL; lives in Richmond, CA

	Mary Mattingly (she/her) b. 1978, Rockville, CT; lives in New York, NY

	Gamaliel Rodriguez (he/him) b.1977, Bayam├│n, Puerto Rico; lives in Cabo Rojo, Puerto Rico

	Deirdre OŌĆÖMahony (she/her) b. 1956, Limerick, Ireland; lives in Cork City, Ireland
	

	Abraham O. Oghobase (he/him) b.1979, Lagos, Nigeria; lives in Toronto, Canada

	Selma Selman (she/her) b. 1991, Biha─ć, Bosnia and Herzegovina; lives in Amsterdam, Netherlands
